g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of Firmware and Bootloader. These are critical concepts in embedded systems that enable hardware to run applications.
Firmware is specialized software stored in non-volatile memory that provides low-level control for specific hardware components.
A bootloader is a small program in protected memory that executes after reset, initializes hardware, and loads the main application into memory.
The boot process flows from Power On/Reset through the protected Bootloader to the updatable Application, with optional Update Mode.
Firmware has key characteristics: stored in non-volatile memory, directly interacts with hardware, and requires specialized development tools.
There are three main types of firmware: Bare-metal for simple control, RTOS-based for real-time tasks, and Linux-based for complex applications.
Microcontroller memory is organized into distinct regions: Boot Flash, Application Flash, RAM, EEPROM, and the Vector Table.
The bootloader's primary functions include hardware initialization, firmware validation, loading to RAM, and jumping to the application.

g.text: Load application from flash to RAM

g.data-narration: The boot sequence follows six steps: reset, bootloader init, validation, load to RAM, vector table config, then jump to app.
g.text: MCU exits reset → Bootloader initializes peripherals → Validates firmware → Loads to RAM →
Configures vector table offset (VTOR) → Transfers control to application reset handler

g.data-narration: The firmware update process involves receiving new firmware, validating it with checksums, writing to flash, and verifying before reboot.
Bootloaders use various protocols: UART for simplicity, I2C for embedded systems, SPI for high-speed, USB for modern devices, CAN for automotive.
Flash memory requires sector-based erasure before writing, has limited write cycles (10K-100K), and needs specific voltage sequences.
A UART bootloader for ARM Cortex-M initializes clock and UART, waits for update commands, downloads firmware packets, writes to flash, then jumps.
A bootloader command packet contains START, CMD, LEN, DATA, and CHKSUM fields with commands for erase, write, jump, and reboot.
The application jump requires disabling interrupts, setting stack pointer from app vector, and branching to the reset handler address.
In summary: firmware provides low-level hardware control, bootloader enables field updates, memory is organized in distinct regions, and security is critical.
g.text: Firmware: Specialized software in non-volatile memory for hardware control
Bootloader: Protected program that loads and validates application firmware
Memory: BOOTLOADER → APPLICATION → DATA (must not overlap)
Boot Sequence: Reset → Init → Validate → Load → Jump

g.data-narration: For exams: remember bootloader vs firmware distinction, memory map, boot sequence order, flash properties, and watchdog handling.
g.text: • Wrong SP setup • No validation • Memory overlap • Missing VTOR
Draw memory maps, practice jump code, know boot order
